name: Proto-Maghrebi Arabic

ancestors.notes: Descent from Classical Arabic via 7th–8th c. expansion

notes: Proto-Maghrebi Arabic — abstract ancestor node for Moroccan, Algerian, Tunisian, Libyan, and Hassaniya Arabic. Key shared Maghrebi features: vowel reduction/deletion (short vowels → schwa [ə] or Ø, yielding heavy consonant clusters — the defining Maghrebi trait); ج → /ʒ/ (voiced postalveolar fricative, sedentary Maghreb) not /dʒ/; ث → /t/; ذ → /d/; ق → /q/ preserved in sedentary/pre-Hilali varieties (/ɡ/ in Bedouin/rural); Berber (Amazigh) substrate reinforcing pharyngeals and emphatics and licensing the cluster phonotactics. ORTHOGRAPHIC READING BASE: graphemes/positional_graphemes inherit from the modern MSA leaf `ar` (not Classical `arb`), because the modern spoken varieties are written in MSA orthography — so they must read it the way `ar` does: the onset-glide digraphs يَ/وَ (/ja/,/wa/, not diphthongs), the hamza carriers أ/إ as bare /ʔ/ before an explicit harakah, word-final ي/و preferring the long-vowel reading, and pausal tāʾ marbūṭa. Genealogy is unchanged (parent/ancestors still point at Classical); only the data-inheritance base moves. Each variety's own phonological overrides (interdentals, qāf reflex, affrication) sit in its own tables and continue to win over the inherited layer.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: Read (full open-access PDF; printed pages). p.16: Maghribi jīm has no initial occlusive element, realized /ž/ [ʒ]. p.15: sedentary interdental→dental-stop merger. p.17: *q maintained (sedentary) vs /ɡ/ (Bedouin). p.13: unstressed-vowel deletion → initial clusters.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties

location.notes: Glottolog's computed centroid for the North African Arabic family — a summary of a whole clade's range, not a place anyone speaks.
